AT WORK WITH: Andrew Selvaggio;Feeding Olympians And Other Fans Of the Big Mac
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IF you were to guess that the man most responsible for the care and feeding -- O.K., feeding -- of the 15,000 athletes, coaches and officials at the centennial Olympic Games was the same pudgy kid dressed as a clown who would wave customers into Dorkie's Beef and Egger; if you were to guess that this man  was now the executive chef of the McDonald's Corporation; even if you imagined McDonald's had such a thing as a chef, well, you'd probably deserve a break today. 

     The position of McDonald's executive chef is what Andrew Selvaggio found himself considering in 1994 when a friend suggested that he audition for the post. At the time, Mr. Selvaggio had forsaken his culinary roots at central Illinois establishments like Jim's Snack Shop, the Indian Manor, Plentywood Farm and, of course, Dorkie's -- the Selvaggio family's breakfast emporium in McHenry, Ill. He had then gone on to the award-winning Steven restaurant in Scottsdale, Ariz., and the Sheraton in Santa Bar-bara, Calif., before rising to over- see Chicago's exquisite Pump Room.

Tearing Out Vines to Plant Hops
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The Benziger wine family is ripping out prime merlot grapevines in the heart  of the wine country to plant hops, the prickly vine that produces the aromatic buds used in making beer. 

     The Benzigers are branching into beer, establishing a microbrewery called the Sonoma Mountain Brewing Company, on 20 acres along Sonoma Highway in Glen Ellen, about 50 miles north of San Francisco. The brewery, the first in the county to plant hops on a large scale, is expected to open in January on a vineyard and former ostrich ranch below Arrowood Winery.

CHRONICLE
An Abundance, Free for the Picking
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IN the 1940's, when I was growing up, Glenn Miller's hit song "Moonlight Cocktail," a musical recipe that directed you to take a "coupl'a jiggers of moonlight and add a star," told me that life offered more enchanting food and drink than my daily lunchtime cheeseburger, fries and chocolate shake. Four decades later, at 50, when I began a solitary life on an island off the coast of Maine in a seaside cabin without electricity, plumbing, phone or road, I discovered a cuisine as enchanting, and delectable, as moonlight. 

     On the island, my sole source of water is the rain, guttered to a cistern below the cabin and connected to a hand pump at the kitchen sink. Since the island's only store is nearly an hour's walk away, I decided early to use the wild foods everywhere around me as my main source of nourishment. With the help  of books, chiefly those by Euell Gibbons, I eventually learned to gather and prepare more than 50 edible species of wild greens and fruits, seaweeds and shellfish, all growing in lush profusion within a 10-minute radius of my cabin.  From spring through October, I find abundant, delicious bounty free for the picking.

As Budget Cuts Take Hold, the Growth of History at CUNY Is History
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Baruch College's catalogue has a section that Myrna Chase, the chairwoman of  the history department, calls the "dead letters" section -- courses that will not be offered for at least the next two years. Next year, the list will triple  in size, with the loss of such popular subjects as "The Constitutional Era," "Civil War and Reconstruction" and "The Immigrant in American History." 

     "At the last registration, it was so pathetic to see these students not being able to put together a program," Ms. Chase said.

A Weed by Another Name: Salad
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THERE'S an old saying that a weed is a green growing where you don't want it. Although this is a bit of an exaggeration, there is no doubt that one culture's  tasty salad ingredient is another's noxious pest. 

     We received a firsthand education in the weed/green question one Sunday afternoon last summer. Our Turkish friend, Ihsan Gurdal, who was raised in Istanbul, had volunteered to help us weed the vegetable garden at a friend's house. The minute he walked through the garden gate, though, he was transformed  from weedkiller to green-gatherer. The most rampant of the weeds we were busily  ripping out of the ground, he claimed, was actually purslane, an excellent edible green. Despite (or perhaps because of) our skepticism, he picked some, washed it and recreated the deliciously tart salad his family used to enjoy as an afternoon snack.

Tough Justice for Juveniles
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Should we combat the problem of youth violence by sending more children to adult prison? The answer is simple: We're asking the wrong question. 

     Our national fixation with meting out adult punishments to young criminals has blinded us to the underlying crisis -- the juvenile system's shocking inability to impose meaningful penalties, or even supervision, on offenders before they become the "predators" we so fear.

Worse Than Communists
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I'm often asked: "Why aren't you afraid of Gennadi Zyuganov? You suffered under the Communists yourself. Have you forgotten that you were sentenced to a labor camp for your literary work?" 

     On the threshold of the presidential election in Russia, many people are inclined to a near hysterical fear of Mr. Zyuganov, the Communist leader, and are doing everything to inflate the dangers that would threaten the country in the event of his victory: an Iron Curtain, censorship, the gulag, persecution of dissidents. Fear of the Communists is pushing the intellectuals into embracing Boris Yeltsin -- forcing them to choose what would seem the lesser of two evils.

The Fortress Within
nytimes_articles.abstract:
We Israelis woke up yesterday morning and saw the stuff of which our dreams are made -- for better and for worse. At the time of this writing, I still do not know the final election results, but I do know one thing: After three years  in which Yitzhak Rabin and Shimon Peres tried to lead Israel into a different future, into an era of peace, it is clear that while our heads may have surged forward, our heels are still dug deep into the ground. 

     At a certain moment in his life, Mr. Rabin looked two steps ahead and decided that Israel was strong enough to take a calculated risk. He shook Yasir Arafat's hand.
